core_intuitions: LLMs have no data/instruction boundary: the system prompt, user message, and every tool result live in one flat token stream weighted through the same attention, with no privileged instruction register the model can cryptographically verify — so any text the model reads can become an instruction.

Indirect injection is stored-XSS for agents: the payload is planted once in the environment (web page, doc, email, DB row) and fires later on an innocent user's agent — structurally more dangerous than direct injection because the attacker never touches the user's query.

The lethal trifecta: a complete exfiltration requires private data AND untrusted content AND an exfiltration channel to co-occur (logical AND); breaking any one leg stops the breach, and sandboxing the channel (no network) is the most reliable leg to cut.

Jailbreaks attack a different layer than injection — the model's trained refusal values (RLHF/alignment), not its context framing — which is why the two defense families are complementary, not interchangeable.

Jailbreak mechanics: GCG performs gradient descent on DISCRETE tokens (use the gradient to RANK candidate token swaps, then evaluate) and the found suffix transfers white-box to black-box; many-shot overrides the RLHF prior via in-context learning, making a larger context window counterintuitively a larger attack surface.

Defense is multiplicative depth: five imperfect layers (alignment, input filtering, architecture/dual-LLM, output filtering, monitoring) plus structured-output isolation compound to orders-of-magnitude protection — no single layer is a security boundary.

existing.name: injsec-trust-boundary-context
existing.issue: The dashed 'trust boundary (convention only — no delimiter the model can verify)' caption overlaps the descender text of the User Message box, and the bottom caption ('Any segment ... can trigger the downstream tool call') collides with the 'Tool Call / Action' box. In dark theme the bottom collision is severe — the caption text overprints the box. Reading order breaks at both seams.
existing.improvement: Give the trust-boundary a clean horizontal dashed rule sitting in its OWN gap between the User Message box and the first Tool Result box, with its label on a clear line (not overlapping either box). Increase viewBox height and add vertical separation so the bottom caption sits fully below the 'Tool Call / Action' box with no overlap in either theme. Verify the '(direct injection)' text inside the User Message box does not touch the boundary caption. Keep the strong core idea (one flat stream, trusted-by-convention vs untrusted-from-environment, single attention with no privileged register).

existing.name: injsec-lethal-trifecta-exfil
existing.issue: Hub-and-spoke of the three legs, each paired with its 'cut leg' defense, plus the numbered attack-sequence strip along the bottom — reads clearly and correctly in both themes. Foundational idea (logical AND of three conditions, break any leg) lands immediately.

existing.name: injsec-defense-five-layers
existing.issue: Funnel of five layers with multiplicative pass-through rates, the porous-vs-enforceable bracket, and the compounded 0.00012 result at the bottom. Legible and correct in both themes; conveys 'layers multiply, not add' precisely. No change needed.

Return JSON schema only.') which overflows the box's right edge and overprints the central 'Structured Output' box. The connector labels ('Validated / schema', 'struct fields') also collide with the Structured Output box, so the entire middle third is illegible in both themes.

Widen the horizontal gap between the reader box and the Structured Output box, and place the arrow label ('validated fields only') centered on the connector without touching either box. Ensure the Structured Output schema block ({title, main_topics, key_facts, sentiment} — no free-form field) fits fully inside its box. Preserve the three-zone layout (untrusted source -> quarantined zero-tool reader -> privileged orchestrator with tools), the 'raw text never crosses' boundary line, and the 'injection lands here, corrupts output only' annotation.

new_figures.name: injsec-injection-vs-jailbreak
new_figures.concept: Two distinct attack surfaces on an LLM agent: prompt injection hits the context/instruction-following layer, jailbreaks drill into the trained-values (RLHF refusal) core — so the two defense families are complementary.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's whole taxonomy hinges on 'Jailbreaks target a different layer ... the model's trained behavior rather than its context framing,' yet nothing visualizes this organizing distinction. Grasping WHICH mechanism each attack hits is what makes the reader understand why architectural isolation stops injection but not jailbreaks, and why alignment training helps jailbreaks but 'does not address indirect injection at all.' This is the single most foundational unillustrated idea in the chapter.
new_figures.placement: Section '## Jailbreak Taxonomy', right after the intro sentence 'The goal is to elicit outputs that the model's fine-tuning or RLHF training was designed to prevent.'
new_figures.brief: Center: a single LLM agent drawn as two concentric conceptual layers — an OUTER shell labeled 'context window / instruction-following (data and instructions share one token stream)' wrapping an INNER core labeled 'trained values (RLHF refusal prior / learned alignment)'. FROM THE LEFT, a bold 'PROMPT INJECTION' arrow strikes only the OUTER shell; its label reads 'attacks WHAT the model reads — plants text the model treats as instructions.' Hang two small sub-tags off it: 'Direct — the user is the attacker (Ignore previous instructions...)' and 'Indirect — planted in the environment, fires on an innocent user's agent (stored-XSS analogy).' FROM THE RIGHT, a bold 'JAILBREAK' arrow pierces THROUGH the shell into the CORE; its label reads 'attacks the model's LEARNED refusals — overrides trained values.' Hang sub-tags: 'role-play (DAN)', 'fictional framing', 'adversarial suffix (GCG)', 'base64 / encoding', 'many-shot priming'. BOTTOM band, two columns contrasting the defenses each surface needs: left 'Injection -> architectural isolation, least privilege, break the lethal trifecta, dual-LLM' ; right 'Jailbreak -> alignment / adversarial training, output classifiers'. One-line takeaway across the very bottom: 'Different layers -> complementary, not interchangeable, defenses.' Theme-safe (use the untrusted/red family for attack arrows, neutral for the agent, trusted/green for defenses, matching the sibling figures); no hardcoded page colors; small illustrative integers only; must be fully legible as a static frame with a clear left-to-right, outer-to-inner reading order and no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: injsec-gcg-suffix-search
new_figures.concept: The GCG loop: how a gradient-based jailbreak does 'gradient descent on discrete tokens' — a locked harmful prefix plus a mutable adversarial suffix, optimized to make the model start with a compliant 'Sure, here is...'.
new_figures.rationale: This is the most technically detailed passage in the chapter (a loss equation, a 3-step algorithm, and ~50 lines of code) with no visual. The non-obvious intuition — you cannot step discrete tokens along a gradient, so you use the gradient only to RANK candidate swaps and then EVALUATE them — is exactly what a labeled diagram makes click, along with the sobering transfer property (white-box suffix -> black-box model).
new_figures.placement: Section '### Adversarial Suffix Attacks (GCG)', right after the enumerated 3-step optimization list (steps 1-3) and before the Python code block.
new_figures.brief: TOP row — the input construction as a token strip: [ harmful instruction prefix, drawn with a small lock icon = FROZEN ] + [ adversarial suffix = k mutable token slots, each a small box showing a placeholder token like '! } { @' ] feeding a forward pass into the model, whose target continuation is a FIXED green-tinted block 'Sure, here is ...' annotated 'loss L = -log p(target | prefix, suffix)'. BELOW, the optimization as a 3-stage CYCLE around a circular 'repeat' arrow: Stage 1 'Backprop: gradient of L w.r.t. each suffix token's one-hot -> a k x |V| gradient table'; Stage 2 'Per slot, take the top-B token swaps with steepest descent (show 2-3 candidate tokens highlighted under one slot)'; Stage 3 'Evaluate a batch of candidate suffixes, KEEP the lowest-loss one' — then loop. Illustrate that only the suffix mutates: show one suffix slot flipping to a new token between iterations, and a small down-trending loss marker across iterations (a schematic descending step, NO numeric axis, NO benchmark numbers). A side annotation captures the key idea: 'Discrete tokens can't be nudged along a gradient -> use the gradient to RANK swaps, then actually evaluate them.' BOTTOM takeaway strip: 'A suffix optimized on an open-weight model often TRANSFERS to closed models — white-box attack -> black-box deployment.' Keep the prefix locked and target fixed throughout; only the k suffix slots change. Theme-safe; small illustrative integers only (k, B); everything inside the viewBox with a clear top-to-bottom then clockwise reading order.

new_figures.name: injsec-many-shot-prior-override
new_figures.concept: Many-shot jailbreaking: as the number of fabricated compliant examples in context grows, in-context learning overrides the RLHF refusal prior — so a bigger context window is a bigger attack surface.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter states a genuinely counterintuitive core claim — 'larger context windows are, counterintuitively, a larger attack surface' and 'attack strength follows a power law in the number of shots' — with no visual. Seeing the context column fill with fake compliant Q&A while a behavior gauge slides from REFUSE to COMPLY crystallizes the meta-learning intuition (prior gets overridden as n grows) far better than prose.
new_figures.placement: Section '### Many-Shot Jailbreaking', after the paragraph ending 'As n grows, the prior from RLHF training is increasingly overridden.'
new_figures.brief: LEFT: a tall 'context window' column filling top-to-bottom with fabricated Q&A pairs, each showing a harmful-style question and the model APPARENTLY complying (attacker-authored, mark them clearly as 'fabricated'), stacked as n shots; annotate 'no gradient access needed — pure in-context priming.' RIGHT: a vertical behavior gauge / slider that reads REFUSE at the top (labeled 'RLHF prior wins, few shots') and COMPLY at the bottom (labeled 'in-context pattern wins, many shots'), with the pointer shown sliding downward as the column fills. BELOW: a small SCHEMATIC monotone curve, x-axis 'number of shots n' (ticks unlabeled or log-spaced with NO numbers), y-axis 'P(harmful compliance)' (NO numbers), rising then saturating, annotated 'power-law in n (schematic — illustrative only).' Two contrasting mini-states framing the curve: 'n small -> refuse' and 'n large -> comply.' BOTTOM callout: 'Counterintuitive: a bigger context window is a bigger attack surface.' Theme-safe (untrusted/red family for the fabricated shots, neutral for the gauge); strictly NO benchmark numbers or fabricated rates on any axis; everything legible inside the viewBox as a static frame.

summary: The chapter is well served on the INJECTION and DEFENSE side (trust-boundary, lethal-trifecta, five-layer funnel, dual-LLM) but two of those four figures have rendering defects and the entire JAILBREAK half of the chapter is unillustrated. Verdicts: keep the trifecta and five-layer figures (both clean and correct in light and dark); refine the trust-boundary figure (its trust-boundary caption overlaps the User Message box and its bottom caption collides with the Tool Call box, badly in dark) and the dual-LLM figure (the reader box's body text overflows and overprints the central Structured Output box, making the middle third illegible). Add three new figures for the jailbreak section's core intuitions: (1 must-have) injection-vs-jailbreak as two attack surfaces hitting different layers, explaining why the defense families are complementary; (2) the GCG discrete-token gradient loop with the transfer property; (3, nice-to-have) many-shot prior override showing bigger context = bigger attack surface. All new figures are static — each core mechanism is fully conveyed by a single labeled frame — and stay within theme-safety and no-invented-numbers constraints.
